title: Social Fiction Design Competition 2026: Win USD 10,000 by Imagining a Better World for Young People in 2050
summary: Young writers, dreamers, storytellers, and changemakers from around the world are invited to participate in the Social Fiction Design Competition 2026, a global creative writing competition that challenges participants to imagine a better, fairer, and more sustainable world for young people in the year 2050. Organized by Yunus Center and powered through YY Ventures , this international initiative encourages people everywhere to use the power of imagination and storytelling to envision solutions to today’s social and environmental challenges. The competition is open globally with no age restrictions and no geographical limitations, making it one of the most inclusive international creative writing opportunities currently available. Participants stand a chance to win a grand prize of USD 10,000 while contributing to conversations about the future of humanity, sustainability, innovation, and social transformation. What is the Social Fiction Design Competition? The Social Fiction Design Competition is an international writing competition focused on “Social Fiction” — a form of imaginative storytelling similar to science fiction, but centered on social innovation and human progress rather than futuristic technology alone. The competition invites participants to imagine: What the world could look like in 2050 How young people may live in the future How societies can overcome inequality and injustice What sustainable communities might become How governments, businesses, and communities can work together for positive change The goal is to inspire new ways of thinking about social innovation and encourage people to imagine the world they truly want to build. According to Nobel Peace Laureate Muhammad Yunus : “If we do not imagine, it will not be done. If we imagine, someday it will happen.” This powerful idea forms the foundation of the competition. What is Social Fiction? Social Fiction is a storytelling approach that imagines a future society shaped by positive social transformation. Unlike traditional science fiction, which often focuses heavily on futuristic machines or scientific advancement, Social Fiction focuses on: Human-centered innovation Social justice Sustainability Equality Community transformation Ethical development Inclusive economies Environmental healing The concept encourages writers to creatively explore solutions to real-world problems and imagine societies where communities thrive. The organizers believe that imagination can inspire real-world innovation and policy change. Competition Theme: The Life of a Young Person in 2050 Participants are asked to imagine and describe: “How do you wish to see the life of a young person in the year 2050?” Writers are encouraged to take readers on a journey into a future world that is free from the social and environmental injustices affecting communities today. The competition emphasizes optimism, innovation, and bold thinking. Applicants should imagine a future where systems, institutions, and communities have evolved to create better opportunities and improved quality of life for younger generations. Writing Requirements Participants must submit an original Social Fiction piece between: Minimum: 1,000 words Maximum: 5,000 words The writing should creatively explore a fictional world in 2050 while addressing one or more major social or environmental issues. Suggested Focus Areas Participants may choose to focus on one or several of the following areas: Climate Change Agriculture Technology and Innovation Micro-credit Transport Literature Music Waste Management Circular Economy Tourism WASH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) Employment Sports Health and Well-being Community Development Sustainability Social Equity The organizers encourage participants to think broadly and creatively about how these sectors could evolve positively by 2050. What Should Your Story Include? The competition encourages writers to explain how different stakeholders contribute to building a better future. Stories may include the role of: Governments Educational institutions Corporate organizations Local businesses Community leaders Entrepreneurs Youth movements Civil society organizations Participants should demonstrate how collaboration and innovation help shape a more just and sustainable society. Why This Competition Matters The Social Fiction Design Competition is not just a writing contest. It is a platform for global imagination and social innovation. At a time when the world faces challenges such as: Climate change Rising inequality Unemployment Environmental degradation Mental health concerns Economic instability Social exclusion The competition seeks bold visions of hope and possibility. It encourages people to move beyond criticism and begin imagining actionable futures worth building. Prize Opportunity: Win USD 10,000 One of the major attractions of the competition is the opportunity to win: USD 10,000 The prize recognizes outstanding creativity, originality, optimism, and social imagination. Beyond financial rewards, participants gain international exposure and become part of a global conversation on innovation and sustainable futures. Who Can Participate? One of the strongest features of the Social Fiction Design Competition is its accessibility. There are: No age restrictions No geographical limitations This means anyone from anywhere in the world can participate. Students, writers, professionals, activists, researchers, entrepreneurs, and aspiring storytellers are all encouraged to apply. Can You Apply More Than Once? According to the competition FAQ: Multiple applications are not permitted. Applicants are encouraged to focus on submitting one strong and well-developed Social Fiction piece. Is There an Application Fee? The competition states that there is: No application fee Participation is completely free. Can Teams Apply? The FAQ section indicates that applicants should review the application guidelines regarding team participation. Participants are encouraged to verify current submission rules through the official competition platform. Previous Winners and Finalists The competition has already attracted talented participants from different countries around the world. Previous Winner Amit Kumar Mishra – Nepal Previous Finalists Neha Sabu – India Wan Faizal Mohd – Malaysia Anne Julienne Barcelona – United States Tyfur Rahman – Bangladesh The diversity of past finalists demonstrates the truly international nature of the competition. Why You Should Apply Participating in the Social Fiction Design Competition can help writers and changemakers: Showcase creativity globally Build visibility as a thought leader Contribute to social innovation conversations Inspire real-world solutions Develop storytelling and writing skills Gain recognition for visionary ideas Connect imagination with impact For young innovators, students, activists, and creative thinkers, this is a unique opportunity to influence future thinking through storytelling. How to Apply Applicants are required to: Write an original Social Fiction piece Ensure the submission falls within 1,000–5,000 words Complete the online application form Submit the story through the official competition platform For inquiries: Email: info@yy.ventures WhatsApp: +880 1648-397420 Apply Here For more information on this competition visit here Discover more opportunities on OFY here The post Social Fiction Design Competition 2026: Win USD 10,000 by Imagining a Better World for Young People in 2050 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Global AgriInno Challenge 2026: Fully Funded International Opportunity for Agrifood Innovators Solving Climate and Food Challenges in Small Island Developing States
summary: The Global AgriInno Challenge (GAC) 2026 is now officially open for applications, offering an exceptional international platform for startups, agripreneurs, researchers, and innovation teams developing transformative agrifood solutions for Small Island Developing States (SIDS). Co-organized by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations , Zhejiang University , and Pinduoduo , the Global AgriInno Challenge has become one of the world’s leading youth-focused agrifood innovation competitions. The initiative connects emerging innovators with investors, policymakers, researchers, global experts, and technical partners to accelerate impactful solutions for sustainable food systems. The 2026 edition specifically focuses on Small Island Developing States (SIDS), which face increasing threats from climate change, food insecurity, biodiversity loss, geographic isolation, and limited innovation ecosystems. Despite these vulnerabilities, many SIDS remain underserved in global investment and technology opportunities, making this challenge particularly significant. Applications are open globally until 20 June 2026 (24:00 CEST) . What is the Global AgriInno Challenge 2026? The Global AgriInno Challenge is an international innovation competition designed to identify, support, and scale technology-driven agrifood solutions that can strengthen resilience and sustainability in vulnerable island regions. The challenge seeks innovations capable of transforming food systems while addressing pressing environmental, economic, and social issues affecting island communities worldwide. GAC 2026 emphasizes both “blue” and “green” agrifood systems, encouraging solutions related to: Agriculture Fisheries Aquaculture Climate resilience Sustainable food production Nutrition Biodiversity conservation Rural livelihoods Youth employment Digital agriculture The competition creates opportunities for innovators to gain international visibility, receive mentorship and coaching, network with industry leaders, and pitch their solutions on a global stage. Why the Focus on Small Island Developing States (SIDS)? Small Island Developing States are among the regions most vulnerable to: Rising sea levels Extreme weather events Climate-related disasters Dependence on imported food Limited agricultural land Fragile ecosystems Economic vulnerability Youth unemployment Weak innovation infrastructure At the same time, SIDS possess enormous potential for sustainable innovation, climate-smart agriculture, fisheries transformation, renewable systems, and community-based development. The Global AgriInno Challenge aims to support practical innovations that respond directly to the realities of island life and create long-term resilience for local communities. Challenge Themes for Global AgriInno Challenge 2026 Applicants must develop or scale solutions aligned with at least one of the four key pillars critical to SIDS resilience. 1. Better Production This pillar focuses on strengthening food production systems despite environmental and geographic limitations. Innovations may address: Limited arable land Climate pressures on agriculture Fisheries sustainability Food-import dependency Fragmented supply chains Precision agriculture Smart farming technologies Sustainable aquaculture Digital farming systems Solutions that improve productivity while promoting sustainability are highly encouraged. 2. Better Nutrition This theme focuses on improving access to affordable, healthy, and nutritious diets. Applicants may propose innovations that tackle: Malnutrition Obesity Diet-related non-communicable diseases (NCDs) Food affordability challenges Nutrition education Food quality Local food systems The challenge encourages scalable approaches that can improve public health outcomes in island communities. 3. Better Environment Environmental protection is a major priority for SIDS due to their highly vulnerable ecosystems. Relevant innovation areas include: Coral reef protection Coastal ecosystem restoration Biodiversity conservation Climate adaptation Sustainable resource management Forest conservation Ocean sustainability Climate-smart technologies Solutions should demonstrate measurable environmental impact and long-term sustainability. 4. Better Life This pillar focuses on improving livelihoods and inclusive economic development. Applications may address: Rural development Youth unemployment Gender inclusion Digital inclusion Economic empowerment Entrepreneurship Job creation Community resilience The challenge seeks innovations capable of improving quality of life and expanding opportunities for underserved populations. Who Can Apply for the Global AgriInno Challenge 2026? Applications are open to agripreneurs and innovation teams from around the world. To qualify, applicants must meet the following eligibility requirements: Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Be registered as a company or legal entity. Have less than USD 1,000,000 in annual turnover. Have operated for less than 5 years. Possess a technology-based solution or business model at Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 7–9. Provide evidence that the innovation is beyond the MVP (Minimum Viable Product) stage. Have at least one team leader under the age of 35. Commit to active participation throughout the entire programme. Have a solution directly addressing agrifood challenges in SIDS or already piloted in island environments. Important Notes Teams from outside SIDS are fully eligible to apply. Preference will be given to teams originating from or led by individuals from SIDS. Teams with female agripreneurs are strongly encouraged to apply. Only one application per applicant will be accepted. The final submission will be considered the official application. Programme Activities and International Exposure Selected teams will participate in several international activities designed to strengthen their innovations and visibility. Innovation for Good Workshops Finalists will receive coaching, mentorship, and capacity-building support through specialized workshops focused on innovation scaling, pitching, and agrifood entrepreneurship. Global Final Pitch Event in Hangzhou, China Finalists will travel to Hangzhou, China in August 2026 to present their solutions before international experts, investors, policymakers, and stakeholders. This stage offers substantial global exposure and networking opportunities. FAO Science and Innovation Forum Showcase in Rome, Italy Outstanding teams may also be showcased during the FAO Science and Innovation Forum in Rome in October 2026, further increasing international recognition and partnership opportunities. Key Benefits for Finalists and Winners Participants selected for the Global AgriInno Challenge 2026 can expect numerous benefits, including: Global visibility and recognition International networking opportunities Access to investors and technical experts Mentorship and coaching support Participation in global innovation workshops Exposure to policymakers and development partners Opportunities for collaboration and partnerships Inclusion in long-term alumni activities Potential international showcase opportunities The challenge provides a strong platform for scaling innovative agrifood solutions globally. Working Languages The official working languages for GAC 2026 are: English Chinese However: All proposals must be submitted in English. All presentations must also be conducted in English. Global AgriInno Challenge 2026 Timeline Key Dates Date Activity 19 May – 20 June 2026 Open Call for Applications 18 June – 30 June 2026 Preliminary Round Selection 2 July 2026 Announcement of Top 13 Finalists July 2026 Remote Preparation and Coaching August 2026 Innovation for Good Workshops and Global Final Pitch Event in Hangzhou 12 – 16 October 2026 FAO Science and Innovation Forum Showcase in Rome 2027 onwards Alumni Network Activities Why Agrifood Startups Should Apply The Global AgriInno Challenge is more than a competition. It is a strategic opportunity for young innovators to gain access to: International markets Global investors Policy stakeholders Technical mentorship International recognition High-level innovation ecosystems For startups working in climate-smart agriculture, food systems, aquaculture, fisheries, rural livelihoods, nutrition, environmental sustainability, and digital agriculture, this challenge offers a unique platform to scale impact globally. Innovators already working in African island contexts, Caribbean nations, Pacific islands, and other vulnerable coastal environments may find this opportunity especially relevant. Data Protection and Privacy According to the organizers, FAO processes all personal and non-personal data in line with its internal Data Protection Policy and procedures. The organization emphasizes: Fair data processing Transparency Security Integrity Responsible handling of information Applicants are encouraged to review the official privacy policies before submitting applications. How to Apply Interested applicants should review the official application requirements and FAQs carefully before submission. Applications close on: Deadline: 20 June 2026 (24:00 CEST) For further inquiries, applicants may contact: global_agriinno_challenge@zju.edu.cn e-agriculture@fao.org Apply Here For more information on the competition, visit here Discover more opportunities on OFY, click here The post Global AgriInno Challenge 2026: Fully Funded International Opportunity for Agrifood Innovators Solving Climate and Food Challenges in Small Island Developing States appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: NASA Creator Proposals 2026: Opportunity for Filmmakers, Storytellers, Songwriters, Poets and Documentarians to Partner with NASA
summary: NASA has opened an exciting opportunity for creative professionals who want to help tell the stories behind some of the agency’s most ambitious missions and innovations. As NASA continues to push the boundaries of space exploration, aviation research, nuclear propulsion, and lunar missions, the agency is inviting creators to submit proposals for potential partnerships that will help bring these mission stories to wider audiences. This opportunity is designed for filmmakers, documentarians, songwriters, storytellers, poets, and other creative professionals who can develop powerful content around NASA’s work. Through this call, NASA is looking for partners who can communicate the agency’s missions in compelling, accessible, educational, and inspiring ways. NASA published the Announcement for Proposals on May 21 and is inviting eligible creators to submit their proposals by Tuesday, June 30 . About the NASA Creator Proposals Opportunity The NASA Creator Proposals opportunity is part of the agency’s wider effort to share the human stories, technical achievements, scientific goals, and future possibilities connected to its missions. NASA is currently working on major programs that could shape the future of space exploration and aviation. These include the Artemis Moon missions, the development of future lunar infrastructure, nuclear propulsion technologies, missions connected to Mars exploration, and advanced aeronautics research. Through this opportunity, NASA wants creative partners who can help translate these complex missions into stories that the public can understand, appreciate, and engage with. In this initial round, NASA is seeking up to 10 partners through unfunded Space Act Agreements . These partnerships will allow selected creators to work with NASA in sharing stories, insights, and educational content related to selected agency missions and programs. What NASA Is Looking For NASA is looking for creators who can present strong storytelling ideas connected to the agency’s missions and innovation areas. Eligible creative professionals may include: Filmmakers Documentarians Songwriters Storytellers Poets Creative producers Multimedia creators Other content creators with strong storytelling proposals The agency is especially interested in proposals that can help communicate the stories behind NASA’s missions, not just the technical details. This means applicants should think about how their creative work can explain the significance, challenges, human effort, innovation, and future impact of NASA’s work. NASA Mission Areas Covered by This Opportunity NASA has identified several areas of focus for this opportunity. Applicants can submit proposals connected to one or more of these mission areas. 1. Artemis Program and Future Moon Missions One of the major focus areas is NASA’s Artemis program. The Artemis program is NASA’s effort to return humans to the Moon and prepare for future deep space exploration. Through Artemis, NASA aims to establish a long-term human presence on and around the Moon while developing the knowledge, technologies, and systems needed for future missions to Mars. The opportunity mentions several Artemis-related areas, including: The Artemis program as a whole The recently added Artemis III mission in 2027 The Artemis IV lunar landing in 2028 NASA’s plans to develop a Moon Base Broader stories connected to lunar exploration and future human presence on the Moon Creators interested in space exploration, human achievement, science communication, lunar missions, and the future of humanity beyond Earth may find this area especially suitable. 2. Nuclear Propulsion and Mars Exploration NASA is also inviting proposals connected to its advancement of nuclear propulsion. Nuclear propulsion is an important area of future space exploration because it could support faster and more efficient travel beyond Earth orbit. This technology could play a key role in future missions to Mars and other deep space destinations. The announcement specifically mentions NASA’s nuclear propulsion work, including: The Space Reactor-1 Freedom mission to Mars in 2028 The Skyfall payload Broader innovation in nuclear propulsion for future space missions This focus area may be ideal for creators interested in futuristic technology, engineering, Mars exploration, science storytelling, and the next generation of space travel. 3. NASA Aeronautics and Aviation Innovation NASA’s work is not limited to space exploration. The agency is also involved in cutting-edge aviation research and aeronautics innovation. Through flight tests and other aviation-focused efforts, NASA continues to contribute to the future of air travel, aircraft performance, flight safety, sustainability, and advanced aviation technology. Creators may submit proposals focused on NASA’s aviation work, including: Flight tests Aeronautics research Advanced aircraft concepts Innovation in aviation technology Stories behind NASA’s work in improving air travel and flight systems This area may appeal to creators interested in aviation, engineering, technology, climate-conscious transport innovation, and the future of flight. Nature of the Partnership Selected creators will enter into unfunded Space Act Agreements with NASA. This means the opportunity is a partnership arrangement rather than a traditional funded grant. NASA is not offering direct funding under this opportunity. Instead, selected partners may receive collaboration opportunities that could include access to NASA expertise, facilities, personnel, mission insights, or other support depending on the approved proposal and NASA’s needs. Applicants should clearly explain what kind of support they need from NASA to carry out their project. Possible needs may include: Access to NASA facilities Access to NASA personnel Interviews with mission teams Technical background information Mission-related insights Coordination support Visual, educational, or archival resources where available Other specific support required to complete the proposed creative work Applicants should also clearly explain their own funding and distribution plans. What Proposals Should Include NASA has stated that proposals should include clear details about the applicant’s area of focus, funding arrangements, distribution approach, and specific needs from the agency. A strong proposal should likely explain: The mission area of interest Applicants should state whether they want to focus on Artemis, Moon missions, nuclear propulsion, Mars-related innovation, aeronautics, or another NASA mission area. The creative concept Applicants should describe the story they want to tell and why it matters. The format of the project This may include a film, documentary, song, poetry project, storytelling series, digital media project, educational content, or another creative format. The intended audience Applicants should explain who the project is designed for, such as students, general audiences, science enthusiasts, educators, families, young people, or global audiences. Funding arrangements Since this is an unfunded partnership, applicants should explain how the project will be financed or supported. Distribution arrangements Applicants should explain how the final creative work will reach audiences. This may include film festivals, streaming platforms, social media, schools, museums, public events, media outlets, or digital platforms. Specific support needed from NASA Applicants should be clear about any access, interviews, facilities, personnel, or mission information they need from NASA. Production timeline A clear timeline can help NASA understand whether the project is realistic and achievable. Experience and capacity Applicants should show that they have the skills, creative experience, or team capacity needed to deliver the proposed project. Who Can Apply? This opportunity is primarily focused on U.S. creators . However, NASA has indicated that it will consider proposals that include a minority of international participants . This means international creators may be included as part of a proposal, but the opportunity is mainly intended for U.S.-based creators or U.S.-led creative teams. Applicants should review the full requirements carefully before submitting. Why This Opportunity Matters NASA’s missions are some of the most important scientific and technological efforts in the world. However, these missions are not only about rockets, aircraft, spacecraft, engineering systems, or scientific instruments. They are also about people, imagination, discovery, risk, innovation, and the future of humanity. Creative professionals play an important role in helping the public connect emotionally and intellectually with science and exploration. Through storytelling, film, music, poetry, documentary work, and multimedia content, creators can help audiences understand: Why NASA’s missions matter How space exploration benefits humanity What challenges scientists and engineers are solving How aviation and space technology affect everyday life What the future of the Moon, Mars, and deep space exploration may look like How young people can be inspired to pursue science, technology, engineering, arts, and mathematics This opportunity gives creators a chance to help shape how the public experiences and understands NASA’s next chapter. Deadline for Applications The deadline to submit proposals is Tuesday, June 30 . Interested applicants are encouraged to review the full requirements and prepare a detailed proposal before the deadline. Application Link Full requirements and application details are available here: https://go.nasa.gov/CreatorProposals Contact Information For further information, applicants may contact: Camille Gallo NASA Headquarters, Washington Phone: 202-358-1600 Email: camille.m.gallo@nasa.gov Cheryl Warner NASA Headquarters, Washington Phone: 202-358-1600 Email: cheryl.m.warner@nasa.gov Final Thoughts The NASA Creator Proposals 2026 opportunity is a unique chance for creative professionals to partner with one of the world’s leading space and aeronautics agencies. Whether through film, documentary, music, poetry, storytelling, or multimedia production, selected creators will have the opportunity to help communicate the stories behind NASA’s missions and innovations. For creators passionate about space exploration, science communication, aviation, the Moon, Mars, and the future of humanity, this opportunity offers a powerful platform to contribute to public understanding and inspiration. Applicants should prepare strong, clear, and realistic proposals that explain their creative vision, distribution plan, funding arrangements, and the support they would need from NASA. Deadline: Tuesday, June 30 APPLY HERE. For more information about this opportunity click here. Discover more proposal writing opportunities on OFY here. The post NASA Creator Proposals 2026: Opportunity for Filmmakers, Storytellers, Songwriters, Poets and Documentarians to Partner with NASA appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Quantitative Political Science at the University of Oxford (Voter ID Project)
summary: The University of Oxford invites applications for the prestigious role of Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Quantitative Political Science , contributing to the ESRC‑funded Voter ID Project led by Principal Investigator Prof Petra Schleiter . This full‑time, fixed‑term position offers an exceptional opportunity to join one of the world’s most respected political science research environments and contribute to cutting‑edge work on electoral behaviour, voter identification policies, and democratic participation in the United Kingdom. This role is based within the Department of Politics and International Relations (DPIR) , one of the largest and most influential political science departments globally. The successful candidate will work closely with a distinguished research team, including co‑investigators Prof Margit Tavits and Prof Jonathan Homola , and will engage deeply with experimental survey design, advanced quantitative analysis, and policy‑relevant research dissemination. Application Deadline: 12 noon (UK time) on Friday, 26 June 2026 About the Voter ID Project The ESRC‑funded Voter ID Project investigates the effects of introducing mandatory photo identification in the UK—a country without national ID cards. The research integrates multiple data sources and methodologies to understand how voter ID requirements shape political behaviour, attitudes, and participation. The project includes three major components: Original surveys with embedded experiments Designed to measure behavioural and attitudinal responses to voter ID requirements. Historical data from Northern Ireland Examining the introduction of voter ID in the early 2000s. Data from the English voter ID trials (2018–2019) Providing comparative insights into turnout, postal voting, and demographic impacts. A new and significant strand of the project focuses on young voters , including those aged 16–17 , to understand how voter ID policies influence the youngest cohort of current and future voters. Key Responsibilities The Postdoctoral Research Fellow will play a central role across all strands of the project. Responsibilities include: 1. Experimental Research Design and Survey Management Contributing to the design of experimental research. Coding and testing survey instruments. Monitoring data collection and quotas in collaboration with survey firms. Conducting data quality checks. 2. Methodological Development Adapting existing methodologies and developing new ones tailored to the project’s data. Advising team members on methodological and procedural matters. 3. Data Analysis Analysing original survey data. Working with historical voter ID data from Northern Ireland. Examining turnout, postal voting, and related electoral datasets. 4. Dissemination and Policy Engagement Presenting findings at conferences. Contributing to world‑class publications. Preparing policy briefs. Supporting engagement with policymakers, including: The Electoral Commission The Department for Housing, Levelling Up and Communities House of Commons Committee Clerks 5. Project Administration Assisting with grant applications. Organising workshops and conferences. Managing personal research activities and workload. 6. Required Training Upon Appointment New staff must complete training in: Information Security and Data Protection Implicit Bias Bullying and Harassment Equality and Diversity Harassment in Higher Education Selection Criteria Essential Qualifications and Experience Applicants must demonstrate: A completed or near‑completed doctorate in political science or a related field. Experience with survey experiments, survey design, and survey data analysis. Expertise in advanced quantitative methods and research design. Proficiency in R . Strong understanding of UK electoral politics. Ability to publish in leading political science journals. Strong communication and interpersonal skills. Commitment to equality, diversity, and inclusion. Evidence of good citizenship and willingness to contribute to administrative tasks. Ability to critically evaluate recent trends in the field. Desirable Attributes Experience collaborating on research articles for publication. About the University of Oxford The University of Oxford is a global leader in research and education, known for its commitment to innovation, academic excellence, and societal impact. Oxford fosters a diverse, inclusive, and supportive environment where scholars thrive and contribute to global knowledge. The University is: A top institution for research excellence. A leader in entrepreneurship, producing 15–20 spin‑out companies annually. Home to world‑class cultural, academic, and professional resources. About the Social Sciences Division Oxford’s Social Sciences Division is a powerhouse of interdisciplinary research, supporting scholars through: Research development guidance Funding opportunities Project design and management support Career development resources About the Department of Politics and International Relations (DPIR) DPIR is one of the world’s most respected political science departments, ranked #1 in the UK by The Guardian (2024). With around 90 academic staff, the department hosts major research projects, postdoctoral researchers, and a vibrant academic community. The department is committed to: Equality, diversity, and inclusion Applying for the Athena Swan Bronze Award Supporting staff wellbeing Providing a collaborative and dynamic research environment Benefits of Working at Oxford Oxford offers an exceptional benefits package, including: 38 days of paid holiday Generous pension schemes Travel discounts Professional development opportunities Free entry to University museums and gardens Discounted access to sports facilities Childcare support and family‑friendly policies Staff networks for BME, LGBT+, disabled staff, and researchers Support for relocation and visa applications How to Apply Applications must be submitted through Oxford’s online recruitment portal. Applicants must upload: A CV A supporting statement addressing each selection criterion Details of two referees All documents must be submitted as PDFs. Deadline: 12 noon (UK time) on Friday, 26 June 2026 Apply here For more information about this opportunity visit here Discover more job opportunities here The post Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Quantitative Political Science at the University of Oxford (Voter ID Project) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: FEC 2026 Innovation Challenge: Funding and Scaling Africa’s Next Generation of Clean Energy Innovators
summary: The Future of Energy Conference (FEC) 2026 Innovation Challenge is now officially open for applications, inviting young African innovators to showcase breakthrough ideas that can transform the continent’s energy future. This high-impact platform is designed to identify, support, and accelerate solutions in low-carbon energy, clean technologies, and sustainable systems across Africa. Hosted as part of the broader FEC ecosystem, this challenge connects innovators directly with policymakers, investors, development partners, and energy experts committed to accelerating Africa’s green transition. The challenge will culminate at the Final Pitch Event in Accra, Ghana, on 25–26 August 2026 , where selected finalists will present their innovations on a continental stage. About the FEC Innovation Challenge The Future of Energy Conference Innovation Challenge 2026 is a flagship innovation program under the broader Future of Energy Conference (FEC) initiative. It focuses on empowering young African problem-solvers who are building scalable solutions in the energy and climate space. The challenge supports innovations that align with Africa’s urgent need for: Sustainable energy access Clean cooking solutions Energy efficiency systems Circular economy innovations Advanced battery and energy storage technologies This initiative is more than a competition—it is a launchpad for scaling climate and energy startups across Africa. Innovation Tracks Available Applicants can apply under two distinct tracks depending on their innovation focus. Track 1: Pan-African Low-Carbon Innovation Track This track supports broad, scalable energy solutions including: Renewable energy systems Smart and digital energy solutions Industrial energy efficiency technologies Off-grid and decentralized power systems Clean cooking innovations Circular economy energy models This track is ideal for innovators working on practical, scalable, and impactful clean energy solutions. Track 2: Afro-Hackathon Track (Battery Innovation Focus) This specialized track focuses on: Lithium-ion battery design and development Energy storage innovation Battery value chain solutions Advanced energy materials Recycling and sustainability in battery systems This track is supported by major ecosystem partners including: AUDA-NEPAD AMDC Africa Change Lab UNDP Who Can Apply? The Innovation Challenge is designed specifically for young African innovators , whether based on the continent or in the diaspora. Eligible Applicants: Entrepreneurs Engineers and inventors Researchers and students Startup founders Energy innovators and developers Age Requirement: 15 to 35 years old Innovation Stage: Applicants may apply at any stage: Early-stage idea Working prototype Pilot-tested solution Market-ready innovation Selection Process The Innovation Challenge follows a structured multi-stage selection process: Stage 1: Application Review Evaluation of innovation quality and feasibility Assessment of impact potential Stage 2: Virtual Interviews Shortlisted applicants pitch their ideas Technical and impact assessment Stage 3: Innovathon Phase Case-based innovation challenge Problem-solving under real-world scenarios Stage 4: Final Pitch Event Selected finalists present live in Accra, Ghana Interaction with investors and policymakers Benefits for Selected Innovators Participants gain access to a powerful ecosystem of support and visibility: Financial Recognition Support Cash prizes for winners Continental recognition platform Mentorship Incubation Access to expert mentorship Incubation through ACEP’s Climate Innovation Hub High-Level Exposure Pitching to over 1,000 stakeholders including: Policymakers Investors Energy industry leaders Development partners Intellectual Property Protection Innovators retain full ownership of their ideas Important Dates Applications Open: 24 April 2026 Application Deadline: 31 May 2026 Final Pitch Event: 25–26 August 2026 (Accra, Ghana) How to Apply Click here for more and to apply. Why This Opportunity Matters Africa’s energy future depends on innovation that is: Locally developed Scalable Climate-smart Accessible to underserved communities The Future of Energy Conference Innovation Challenge 2026 plays a key role in bridging the gap between ideas and implementation by connecting young innovators to real funding, mentorship, and policy ecosystems. Click here for more global Competition opportunities The post FEC 2026 Innovation Challenge: Funding and Scaling Africa’s Next Generation of Clean Energy Innovators appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: AI x Media Literacy Dialogue Fund 2026: Funding, Training, and Support for Innovative MIL & AI Projects in Southern Africa
summary: The AI x Media Literacy Dialogue Fund 2026 , hosted by DW Akademie and supported by German development cooperation, is a unique opportunity for organizations across Southern Africa to design, test, and implement innovative projects at the intersection of Media and Information Literacy (MIL) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) . This initiative empowers organizations to explore how AI is reshaping media consumption, digital well-being, critical thinking, and community engagement—while equipping them with funding, expert coaching, and a regional network of collaborators. Applications close on 15 June 2026 , and selected organizations will receive up to 2,000 Euros in funding, along with structured training, mentorship, and participation in a regional innovation ecosystem. About the AI x Media Literacy Dialogue Fund The Dialogue Fund supports organizations that want to explore how Media and Information Literacy can help communities navigate the rapidly evolving AI landscape. As AI increasingly influences identity, relationships, emotional well-being, and access to information, MIL becomes essential for helping people—especially youth—engage responsibly and critically with digital technologies. DW Akademie, Deutsche Welle’s center for international media development, leads this initiative. Their mission is to strengthen freedom of expression, access to reliable information, and constructive dialogue worldwide. What Types of Projects Are Eligible? The Dialogue Fund welcomes a wide range of creative, educational, and community-centered ideas. Eligible projects may include: Educational campaigns and materials , such as short video series, posters, or visual explainers Youth-focused educational activities , including workshops, training sessions, or school-based programs Interactive content , such as games, clickable explainers, or digital storytelling tools Educational research and dialogue , exploring how AI affects communities and how MIL can address these shifts Projects using vernacular languages are strongly encouraged, as are ideas that promote reliable, independent information and community well-being. How the Fellowship Works: A Step-by-Step Process The Dialogue Fund follows a structured, supportive process designed to help organizations refine and implement their ideas effectively. 1. Application Phase Organizations pitch their idea through the official application form. A selection panel reviews submissions and chooses the most promising concepts. 2. Masterclass (Virtual) Selected teams participate in a virtual Masterclass during the first week of July. This session includes: Expert-led insights into MIL, AI literacy, and digital well-being Discussions on how AI shapes identity, relationships, and emotional health Exploration of MIL competencies needed for healthy digital engagement The Masterclass is interactive and tailored to the ideas submitted by participating teams. 3. Idea Lab (Virtual Workshop) Held in the second week of July, the Idea Lab allows teams to: Present their ideas to other innovative organizations in the region Apply human-centered design approaches Refine their project plans based on feedback and collaboration 4. Implementation Phase From 15 July to 15 October 2026 , selected organizations implement their projects with: Up to 2,000 Euros in funding Ongoing coaching and technical support A dedicated counterpart for feedback, reflection, and guidance 5. Feedback, Exchange, and Showcase Participants join a regional network of MIL innovators and present their final projects during Global MIL Week (24–30 October 2026) . What Is the Masterclass? The Masterclass is a focused, expert-led session that introduces participants to: Media and Information Literacy AI literacy and its societal impact Digital well-being and mental health in the age of AI How AI influences people’s sense of self, relationships, and emotional balance The session is interactive, discussion-based, and designed to help teams strengthen their ideas before implementation. What Is the Idea Lab? The Idea Lab is a collaborative virtual workshop where teams: Present their project concepts Receive feedback from peers and experts Use human-centered design to refine their approach Strengthen their implementation plan This session ensures that each project is tailored to the needs of its intended audience. How the Financial Fellowship Works Selected organizations receive: Up to 2,000 Euros in funding Coaching and technical support A first installment upon signing the fellowship agreement A final installment after submitting the final report and supporting materials Funding may be used for prototyping, production, research, or other project-related expenses. What Applicants Need to Participate To be eligible and successful, applicants must demonstrate: Commitment from all team members for the full project duration Participation of at least two team members in the Masterclass Participation in the virtual Idea Lab A clear idea for a MIL AI product relevant to their audience Commitment to ethical, reliable, and independent information Access to a smartphone or laptop for virtual sessions Willingness to engage in coaching, feedback, and peer dialogue Application Requirements Applicants must: Be at least 21 years old Be based in Namibia, Malawi, Botswana, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Eswatini, South Africa, or Lesotho Be endorsed by their organization Commit to ethical media and information usage Applications must be submitted through the official form before 15 June 2026 . Timeline Overview 15 June 2026: Applications close First week of July: Masterclass Second week of July: Idea Lab 15 July – 15 October 2026: Project implementation 24–30 October 2026: Showcase during Global MIL Week About DW Akademie DW Akademie is a global leader in media development, journalism training, and knowledge transfer. Their work strengthens freedom of expression and access to reliable information, empowering communities to make informed decisions based on facts and constructive dialogue. To apply and for more information about this opportunity visit here. Discover more opportunities here https://opportunitiesforyouth.org The post AI x Media Literacy Dialogue Fund 2026: Funding, Training, and Support for Innovative MIL AI Projects in Southern Africa appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Leadership Accelerator Powered by AI 2026: A Comprehensive 8‑Week Program for Early‑Career Professionals Worldwide
summary: The Leadership Accelerator Powered by AI, developed by Aspire Institute Inc., is a transformative 8‑week professional development program designed to equip early‑career professionals with the AI fluency, leadership capability, communication strength, and strategic thinking required in today’s rapidly evolving workplace. Running from June 1 – July 27, 2026 , this 30‑hour virtual accelerator blends self‑paced learning, live cohort-based sessions, and hands-on applied experiences to create a deeply immersive leadership journey. This program is not simply an AI course, nor is it a traditional soft-skills training. Instead, it integrates both domains into a unified, practical, and future‑ready curriculum. Participants learn how to use AI as a strategic tool while simultaneously developing the interpersonal, ethical, and managerial skills needed to thrive in modern organizations. Program Overview: What the Leadership Accelerator Offers The Leadership Accelerator Powered by AI is structured to help participants build foundational AI knowledge before progressing into communication, collaboration, negotiation, resilience, and ethical leadership. The program culminates in a capstone project where participants design and present an AI-enabled solution to a real workplace challenge. Program Duration and Format Dates: June 1 – July 27, 2026 Total Hours: 30 hours across 8 weeks Weekly Commitment: Approximately 4 hours per week Format: Virtual, self-paced learning combined with live cohort-based sessions Platform: Delivered through Sana, an AI-native learning management system Cost: $100 per participant Certificate: Signed by Aspire Co-Founders and Harvard Business School faculty Eligibility: Ages 18–35 A stable internet connection and a laptop or device with sufficient memory are recommended to fully access the platform’s interactive components. Inside the 8-Week Curriculum The curriculum is intentionally structured to build progressively, ensuring participants gain both technical and leadership competencies. Week 1: Basics of Artificial Intelligence Participants explore: Types of AI Prompt engineering Responsible AI Bias evaluation Decision-support systems This foundational week ensures learners understand how AI works and how to use it responsibly. Weeks 2–3: Communication, Time Management, and Team Dynamics These modules focus on: Professional communication Productivity systems Collaboration workflows Listening and articulation Managing priorities Delegation Building sustainable systems for high performance Participants learn how AI tools can enhance communication and productivity in real workplace settings. Weeks 4 and 7: Peer-to-Peer Learning and Reflection These weeks emphasize: Self-awareness Interpersonal leadership Reflective exercises Community-based learning Collaborative problem-solving Participants strengthen their ability to lead and collaborate effectively. Weeks 5–6: Resilience, Negotiation, and Ethical Leadership These modules include: Negotiation simulations Ethical decision-making Leading under pressure Navigating ambiguity Applying AI tools to strategic thinking Participants practice real negotiation frameworks using AI simulations before applying them in real conversations. Week 8: Capstone Project and Live Masterclasses Participants: Reflect on their leadership journey Identify a real challenge within their organization or community Design an AI-enabled solution Present their implementation plan to peers and managers This final week integrates all skills learned throughout the program. Industry Leaders Teaching Each Module The Leadership Accelerator is taught by globally recognized leaders, including: Nandkishore “Andy” Kalambi – Angel Investor, Technologist, Founder of NatureCentred AI Demond T. Martin – Co-Founder CEO of WellWithAll Álvaro Rodríguez – Co-Founder of IGNIA, Executive Fellow at Harvard Business School Eleri J. Dixon – 2024 Harvard ALI Fellow, Digital Transformation Leader Sháka Rasheed – General Partner, Open Opportunity Fund Peter “Pete” Chow – CTO of ASSIA Inc., IEEE Fellow Niren Chaudhary – Chairman of Panera Brands, Executive Coach Their combined expertise ensures participants receive world-class instruction grounded in real industry experience. What Makes This Program Unique Unlike traditional programs that focus solely on AI or leadership, the Leadership Accelerator Powered by AI integrates both. Participants learn: When AI enhances decision-making When human judgment is essential How to use AI tools in real scenarios such as negotiation, communication, and team leadership How to build systems that improve productivity and collaboration This approach ensures participants gain practical readiness , not just theoretical knowledge. What Participants Gain Upon completion, participants leave with: Practical ability to apply AI tools to real workplace challenges Prompt engineering skills and ethical evaluation frameworks Strong communication and interpersonal leadership skills Time and energy management frameworks A tested negotiation framework A completed AI-enabled capstone project A certificate signed by Aspire Co-Founders and HBS faculty Why Organizations Invest in This Program Organizations choose this accelerator because it helps them: Develop AI-fluent professionals Employees learn to use AI for communication, research, collaboration, and productivity. Strengthen leadership and communication Participants build the interpersonal and decision-making skills needed to lead effectively. Improve retention and growth potential Investing in early-career talent signals long-term commitment and opportunity. Prepare future-ready contributors The capstone project directly addresses real organizational challenges. Program Impact and Global Reach NPS Score: 68 92% of participants report confidence applying learned strategies 100 countries represented in past cohorts How the Program Is Delivered The accelerator runs on Sana , an AI-native LMS offering: Intelligent content delivery AI coaching 24/7 AI tutor support AI-powered role-play simulations Community learning This ensures a dynamic, interactive, and personalized learning experience. Who Should Enroll For Individuals Ideal for: Early-career professionals aged 18–35 Recent graduates entering the workforce Individuals seeking affordable, world-class leadership development For Organizations Ideal for: Employers with high-volume early-career hiring Universities and academies offering career-readiness programs Organizations with social impact goals Enrollment options include: $100 per participant Volume discounts for groups of 20+ Mixed or private cohorts Bundling options for universities Part of the Aspire Ecosystem The Leadership Accelerator is developed by Aspire Institute, the Harvard-founded nonprofit behind the Aspire Leaders Program (ALP), which has reached over 500,000 first-generation students across 195 countries. Revenue from the Leadership Accelerator helps keep the ALP free for students who need it most. Every enrollment directly supports this mission. Deadline The program runs from June 1 – July 27, 2026 . Early enrollment is recommended due to high demand. Apply here For more information about this opportunity visit here Discover more short course opportunities here The post Leadership Accelerator Powered by AI 2026: A Comprehensive 8‑Week Program for Early‑Career Professionals Worldwide appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Partnership Advisor, Water & Resilience at The Nature Conservancy: A Comprehensive Guide for Global Applicants
summary: The Nature Conservancy (TNC), one of the world’s most influential environmental organizations, is seeking a highly skilled and mission‑driven professional for the role of Partnership Advisor, Water Resilience . This full‑time, remote position offers a unique opportunity to contribute to global conservation efforts by shaping strategies, building partnerships, and influencing development finance institutions (DFIs), governments, and corporate actors. This role sits within TNC’s Impact Finance Markets Division , specifically the Corporate Engagement Business Unit , and plays a central part in advancing the organization’s Resilient Freshwater Pathway . The selected candidate will work across global teams, engage with high‑level partners, and support the integration of nature‑based solutions into financial and corporate systems. Note: This is a 2‑year term‑limited position , with the possibility of extension depending on performance and budget availability. Deadline: The position will close once a sufficient pool of qualified candidates has been identified. Early applications are strongly encouraged. About the Role The Partnership Advisor, Water Resilience will serve as a subject‑matter expert and strategic advisor on complex issues related to water, resilience, and development finance. The role requires working across interdisciplinary teams to influence corporate and public financial institutions toward conservation‑aligned decision‑making. Key Functions of the Role Strategic Leadership and Advisory Advise on strategy development for TNC’s Resilient Freshwater Pathway. Support the creation and implementation of strategies for engaging DFIs and water utilities. Contribute to the development of unified proposals, reports, and strategic documents across TNC’s global network. Partnership Development and Management Build and maintain relationships with governments, corporations, DFIs, NGOs, and community partners. Serve as a relationship manager for specific institutions. Represent TNC in global collaborations such as the International Water Association and WaterWorx/Blue Deal. Project and Program Management Lead multiple projects simultaneously within a complex, matrixed organizational structure. Manage DFI trainings and engagements, including potential subject‑matter expert roles. Participate in negotiations for high‑profile or sensitive agreements. Research, Analysis, and Strategic Planning Conduct research to inform corporate engagement and resilient watershed strategies. Contribute to brainstorming, planning, and decision‑making processes. Track progress toward Resilient Watersheds Intermediate Results. Communications and Representation Prepare and deliver proposals, presentations, memos, concept notes, and reports. Represent TNC at internal and external events, including major global gatherings such as Stockholm World Water Week. Build awareness of TNC’s water‑related work among internal and external stakeholders. Fundraising and Financial Management Develop and implement multi‑year fundraising strategies. Manage budgets, negotiate with vendors, and support financial planning. Help meet fundraising targets aligned with program goals. Required Qualifications Bachelor’s degree in political science, environmental policy, management, or a related field. At least 5 years of experience in development finance institutions, government relations, or the corporate sector. Fluency in English. Strong analytical and project management skills. Demonstrated ability to develop and implement complex projects in unstructured environments. Proven communication, presentation, and negotiation skills. Experience managing multiple projects and implementing strategic program goals. Desired Qualifications Multilingual abilities, especially French. Experience presenting to management‑level corporate audiences. Strong interpersonal skills, diplomacy, and the ability to work in multidisciplinary teams. Experience in drought, flooding, disaster risk management, or private financial institutions. Salary Information For U.S. applicants: Annual base salary range: $95,000 – $124,300 , depending on location, experience, and qualifications. For applicants outside the U.S.: Compensation will be in local currency and aligned with local labor markets. Final pay will depend on qualifications, skills, and experience. About The Nature Conservancy Founded in 1951, The Nature Conservancy is a global nonprofit dedicated to conserving the lands and waters on which all life depends. With operations in 79 countries and territories , over 1 million members , and more than 400 scientists , TNC is one of the world’s most impactful conservation organizations. TNC is committed to: Science‑based solutions Inclusive work environments Global collaboration Flexible work arrangements Competitive benefits tailored to each country The organization values diversity and encourages applicants from all backgrounds. Application Notes Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis. The position will be unposted once a strong candidate pool is identified. Early application is strongly recommended. Applicants must pass TNC’s background screening process. Accommodations for applicants with disabilities are available upon request. Apply here For more information about this opportunity visit here Discover more job opportunities here The post Partnership Advisor, Water Resilience at The Nature Conservancy: A Comprehensive Guide for Global Applicants appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy Community of Fellows Program 2026: Apply for Visiting and Resident Fellowships in Philanthropy
summary: The IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy Community of Fellows Program 2026 is now inviting creative scholars and practitioners to apply for visiting and resident fellowship opportunities designed to advance the field of philanthropy. This fellowship program offers a unique opportunity for professionals, researchers, and thought leaders to work closely with one of the world’s leading institutions dedicated to philanthropic education, research, and leadership. Building on decades of advancing philanthropic knowledge and practice, the school has launched a suite of strategic initiatives focused on the cutting edge of philanthropy. Through the Community of Fellows Program , the school seeks individuals who can contribute original ideas, applied research, professional programming, public engagement, and sector-focused innovation. Applications will be reviewed on a rolling basis beginning June 1, 2026 , and selected projects may begin as early as September 8, 2026 . About the IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy Community of Fellows Program The Community of Fellows Program is an initiative of the IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy , a globally recognized institution dedicated to strengthening the understanding and practice of philanthropy. The program invites fellows to collaborate with faculty and staff on research, program development, professional education, donor and practitioner engagement, and public convenings. Fellows may serve as either visiting fellows or in-residence fellows , depending on the nature of their proposed work, availability, and the fellowship area. The fellowship is designed for individuals who want to contribute meaningfully to the field of philanthropy by producing practical, relevant, and high-impact outcomes. Fellowship Areas The IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy is seeking fellows to engage in research and program development in several strategic areas. Applicants should propose work that aligns with one or more of the following fellowship categories: 1. Philanthropic Advising This area focuses on the growing field of philanthropic advising, including how donors, families, foundations, and institutions make giving decisions. Fellows may explore topics such as donor strategy, advisory models, family philanthropy, ethical advising, wealth and generosity, or the professionalization of philanthropic advisory services. 2. Impact Investing Impact investing is an important and expanding part of modern philanthropy. Fellows interested in this area may examine how capital can be used to generate both financial returns and social impact. Projects may explore investment strategies, measurement frameworks, donor behavior, institutional approaches, or the relationship between philanthropy and mission-driven finance. 3. Higher Education and Philanthropy This fellowship area focuses on the relationship between higher education and philanthropic practice. Fellows may investigate fundraising, institutional advancement, donor engagement, alumni giving, university-community partnerships, or the role of higher education institutions in promoting the common good. 4. Policy and Philanthropy Policy plays a major role in shaping philanthropy. Fellows working in this area may explore laws, regulations, tax policy, public-private partnerships, philanthropic advocacy, nonprofit policy environments, or how philanthropic institutions influence and respond to public policy. 5. Sports and Philanthropy Sports and philanthropy is a growing field that examines how athletes, teams, leagues, foundations, and sports organizations engage in giving and social impact. Fellows may develop projects around athlete philanthropy, sports foundations, community development, youth programs, social justice, or the use of sports as a platform for charitable engagement. What Fellows Will Do Selected fellows will collaborate with faculty and staff at the IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy to develop meaningful work in their chosen area. The fellowship is flexible and may include research, program development, public engagement, and professional education. Fellows may participate in one or more of the following activities: Design and execute applied research Fellows may carry out research projects that lead to practical outcomes for the philanthropy sector. Develop professional development offerings Fellows may contribute to training programs, workshops, executive education, or learning resources for practitioners. Engage with the school’s networks Fellows may connect with practitioners, donors, scholars, nonprofit leaders, and philanthropic institutions through the school’s extensive networks. Create or participate in public convenings Fellows may help design conferences, seminars, panels, discussions, or other public events related to their area of focus. Propose creative projects that advance the field The program welcomes innovative ideas beyond traditional research, as long as the proposed work contributes to philanthropy in a meaningful way. Benefits and Resources Available to Fellows Fellows will have access to a range of institutional resources that can support their research, program development, and professional engagement. The fellowship may provide: Affiliation with the IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy Integration into a relevant team at the school Travel support to Indianapolis or other locations for agreed and approved purposes Access to research resources, including library access A project budget of up to $50,000 for the entire project Opportunity to use additional external funding obtained by fellow candidates Collaboration with faculty, staff, practitioners, and donor networks Access to a globally respected philanthropy education and research environment The support provided will depend on the fellowship type, project focus, proposed activities, and available resources. Fellowship Terms and Duration Fellows may serve as either visiting fellows or in-residence fellows . Fellowship terms may range from: One month Several months Up to one full year The exact duration will depend on the proposed project, fellowship category, and nature of the fellow’s work. Fellows will be supported through school resources and/or external funding obtained by the fellows themselves. Who Should Apply? The Community of Fellows Program welcomes applications from both scholars and practitioners . This means the fellowship is suitable for people from academic, nonprofit, philanthropic, advisory, policy, investment, sports, and professional practice backgrounds. Ideal applicants should bring one or more of the following: Demonstrated expertise through scholarship, practice, or both A clear vision for research or program development A proposed project with tangible, relevant outcomes A strong record of collaboration across sectors A demonstrated interest in using the school’s unique resources Authorization to work in the United States Applicants should be able to show how their proposed work will contribute to the advancement of philanthropy as a field. Application Requirements Interested applicants should submit a complete application package to the Program Manager, Saima Hassan , by email at: lfsopcf@iu.edu Applicants should include the fellowship category and final application in the email subject line. The application package must include: 1. Letter of Interest Applicants must submit a letter of interest of no more than three pages . The letter should describe: The fellowship area being applied for The proposed project The project timeline Expected deliverables The applicant’s approach to research or program development How the project will contribute to the field of philanthropy 2. Curriculum Vitae or Professional Résumé Applicants should include a current CV or résumé showing their academic, professional, research, or practice-based experience. 3. Writing Samples or Portfolio Materials Applicants must submit two writing samples or portfolio materials relevant to the proposed fellowship area. These materials should help demonstrate the applicant’s ability, experience, and suitability for the fellowship. 4. Professional References Applicants must provide the names and contact information of two professional references . Application Timeline Applications will be reviewed on a rolling basis beginning June 1, 2026 . Candidates can expect to hear back within approximately one month of applying . Projects may begin as early as September 8, 2026 . Because applications are reviewed on a rolling basis, interested applicants are encouraged to prepare and submit their materials early. Important Note Before Applying Before submitting a formal application, applicants are strongly encouraged to contact the coordinator with informal inquiries. These inquiries should demonstrate that the applicant has: Carefully reviewed the fellowship opportunity Understood the school’s offerings Considered how their project aligns with the fellowship Thought through how they can use the school’s resources effectively This step can help applicants refine their proposals before formal submission. About the IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy The IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy is the world’s first school dedicated solely to philanthropic education, research, and leadership. Through its academic programs, applied research, professional development offerings, and field-building initiatives, the school equips individuals and organizations to strengthen and advance the common good. The school plays an important role in supporting donors, nonprofits, scholars, practitioners, institutions, and leaders who are working to improve philanthropy and social impact around the world. Why This Fellowship Matters The Community of Fellows Program is more than a temporary academic or professional appointment. It is an opportunity to shape conversations, build knowledge, and develop practical tools for the philanthropy sector. This fellowship is especially valuable for applicants who want to: Advance research in philanthropy Build programs that support practitioners and donors Explore emerging areas of philanthropic practice Collaborate with a leading school of philanthropy Produce tangible outputs that can benefit the sector Engage with networks of scholars, practitioners, and philanthropic leaders By bringing together creative scholars and practitioners, the program aims to strengthen the future of philanthropy through applied knowledge, collaboration, and innovation. How to Apply Applicants should send their materials by email to: lfsopcf@iu.edu The email should be addressed to the Program Manager, Saima Hassan . Applicants should include the fellowship category and final application in the subject line. A complete application should include: A letter of interest of up to three pages A current CV or professional résumé Two writing samples or portfolio materials Names and contact information for two professional references Deadline Applications will be reviewed on a rolling basis beginning June 1, 2026 . Projects may commence as early as September 8, 2026 . APPLY HERE. For more information about this opportunity click here. Discover more fellowship opportunities on OFY here. The post IU Lilly Family School of Philanthropy Community of Fellows Program 2026: Apply for Visiting and Resident Fellowships in Philanthropy appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Demetrios G. Papademetriou Young Scholars Program Internship 2026 (Fall Cycle) – Migration Policy Institute
summary: The Migration Policy Institute (MPI) has officially opened applications for its Demetrios G. Papademetriou Young Scholars Program – Fall 2026 Internship Cycle (September–December) . This prestigious program is designed to train the next generation of migration researchers, policy analysts, and global development professionals through hands-on research experience. This internship provides an exceptional opportunity for students and early-career professionals interested in migration, refugee studies, international policy, and social integration to work directly with world-class experts and contribute to real-world policy research. Overview of the Young Scholars Program The Young Scholars Program is named in honor of MPI’s founding president and is designed to achieve two major goals: Strengthen MPI’s global research output through intern contributions Train emerging professionals in migration research and policy analysis Interns are embedded in research teams and contribute to meaningful, policy-driven work across multiple global migration themes. Research Areas Available for Fall 2026 Interns Interns will work in one of the following specialized policy areas: 1. U.S. Immigration Policy Focuses on: Asylum and refugee systems Border governance and enforcement Legal and labor migration pathways Immigration economics and workforce outcomes Policy reform analysis 2. International Migration Covers: Global comparative immigration systems Refugee resettlement frameworks Migration and development linkages Border management and mobility governance Integration policies across countries 3. U.S. Immigrant Integration Includes: Education systems for immigrant communities Workforce training and adult education Language access policies Local and state integration strategies Early childhood and K–12 education support 4. Latin America and the Caribbean Initiative Focuses on: Migration flows from Central America and Venezuela Regional migration governance systems Integration challenges in host countries Policy responses in Mexico, Colombia, Costa Rica Important Requirement: Spanish proficiency (written and spoken) 5. Refugee and Forced Displacement Initiative Explores: Refugee self-reliance and livelihoods Host-community integration Lived refugee experiences Faith-based and humanitarian responses Displacement policy innovation Internship Responsibilities Selected interns will contribute to high-level research and policy work, including: Writing research drafts and policy briefs Conducting literature reviews and summaries Supporting quantitative and qualitative research Assisting with event reporting and policy hearings Contributing to ongoing MPI research initiatives Light administrative and coordination tasks Internship Format Benefits Duration: 4 months (September–December 2026) Location: Remote or in-person (Washington, DC) Compensation: Biweekly stipend (full-time approx. $1,436; prorated for part-time) Flexible start/end dates depending on assignment Academic credit may be arranged Additional notes: Open to U.S. and international applicants (with work authorization or remote eligibility) Must be at least a junior in college or higher (or equivalent experience) Strong writing and analytical skills required Foreign language skills and research experience are highly valued Important Dates Application Deadline: June 15, 2026 Internship Duration: September – December 2026 How to Apply Click here for more and to apply Required documents: CV/Resume Cover letter (max 2 pages) Writing sample (max 5 pages) Academic transcript Important Note: The program requires authentic work only—AI-generated application materials are not permitted and may lead to disqualification. Why This Internship Matters Interning at MPI offers: Direct exposure to global migration policy work Mentorship from leading migration scholars Real contributions to published research Professional development in policy analysis Networking in Washington, DC policy circles Experience across international migration systems Application Strategy (How to Stand Out) To increase your chances: 1. Tailor your focus area Choose ONE primary research track and align everything (CV + writing sample) to it. 2. Strong writing sample selection Best options: Migration policy essay Refugee studies paper International relations research Data-based policy analysis 3. Cover letter must include: Clear motivation for migration policy Specific MPI research area of interest Evidence of analytical writing ability Any language or regional expertise 4. Highlight these skills strongly: Policy analysis Research synthesis Academic writing Quantitative or qualitative methods Language skills (Spanish is a major advantage for LAC track) Click here for more global internship opportunities The post Demetrios G. Papademetriou Young Scholars Program Internship 2026 (Fall Cycle) – Migration Policy Institute appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .
